markdown.markdown:
Welcome to **Economics 100B: Intermediate Microeconomic Theory — Imperfect Competition and Market Failure**.

This course is the second part of a two-part sequence in Intermediate Microeconomics. In Economics 100A, you learned how consumers maximize utility and how firms maximize profits. In this second part, we build upon these concepts by exploring how firms maximize profits in the face of imperfect competition, how market failures such as asymmetric information and externalities affect choices and welfare, and how consumers and firms optimize when facing uncertainty. You will also learn new analytical tools used by other upper-division Economics courses at UC Davis. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

The course material and assignments are organized by week/topic. Complete each item in the order it is presented (lecture materials → practice → assessment), and use discussion sections and office hours to stay on track. :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}

---

# Your Instructor

![Cartoon picture of Erich Muehlegger](images/cartoon_muehlegger.png)
Erich Muehlegger is a Professor of Economics at the University of California, Davis, and a research associate of the National Bureau of Economic Research. His research interests include industrial organization, public finance, economic regulation, and environmental policy. Much of his recent research studies the role and impact of government policy in decarbonizing the transportation sector. He has taught at UC Davis since 2014 and at Harvard Kennedy School from 2005-2014.

Email: emuehlegger@ucdavis.edu  
---

# How to succeed in this course

## Lectures
It is necessary to attend lecture to do well in the course. Lecture notes that preview the material will be posted on Canvas before class. The material for this course and exams will be self-contained within the lecture notes and the lectures themselves. :contentReference[oaicite:8]{index=8}

## Sections
I strongly recommend you attend sections. Sections will be used to discuss problem set and exam solutions as well as supplement lecture material with additional examples. :contentReference[oaicite:9]{index=9}

## Office hours
If you have questions about the material or your performance in the course, drop into office hours. :contentReference[oaicite:10]{index=10}

---

# Course materials and tools

## Recommended textbook
*Microeconomics: Theory and Applications with Calculus*, by Jeffrey M. Perloff (5th Edition used, but any edition is fine). :contentReference[oaicite:11]{index=11}

## In-class polling
I use in-class polling in almost every lecture using iClicker (available at no cost). :contentReference[oaicite:12]{index=12}

## Canvas and announcements
All course materials (lecture notes, problem sets, solutions, sample exam questions/solutions) will be distributed via Canvas. Important information will be posted on Canvas and sent to the class list; you are responsible for checking your UCD registered email. :contentReference[oaicite:13]{index=13}

---

# Grading

Your course grade is based on:
- **Final Exam:** 35%  
- **Midterm Exam:** 25%  
- **Weekly Canvas Quizzes:** 20%  
- **Weekly Problem Sets:** 20% :contentReference[oaicite:14]{index=14}

---

# Exams

All exams are **in person** and **closed book**. :contentReference[oaicite:15]{index=15}

## Midterm
The midterm covers all material up to and including the class just before the midterm.  
**Midterm (all sections):** Tuesday May 6th, in class. :contentReference[oaicite:16]{index=16}

## Final
The final is comprehensive, with added emphasis on post-midterm material.  
**Final (A01–A04):** Tuesday, June 10th at 8am  
**Final (B01–B04):** Monday, June 9th at 8am :contentReference[oaicite:17]{index=17}

Exams must be taken as scheduled except in the case of hospitalization or family emergency. SHCS no longer provides documentation to excuse students from exams for routine illness. Students must notify me prior to the beginning of an exam unless impossible. If you have an excused absence, remaining requirements will be re-weighted; unexcused absences result in an exam grade of zero. :contentReference[oaicite:18]{index=18}

---

# Weekly quizzes

Each Friday (beginning the first week), you will complete a short (10 minute) 5-question multiple choice Canvas quiz, available from 12am–11:59pm Pacific. You must take the quiz by yourself without aid from other students, but may consult your class notes. There is no quiz the week of the midterm. No make-up quizzes are offered; the lowest quiz score is dropped. :contentReference[oaicite:19]{index=19}

---

# Problem sets

Weekly problem sets are due ten minutes after the beginning of class on the designated day and must be submitted on Canvas (no hard copies). Problem sets are graded using a “check-plus/check/check-minus” completion assessment; sincere effort can receive full credit even with mistakes. Incomplete or late problem sets are accepted for partial credit. The lowest problem set grade is dropped. You are encouraged to work in small groups, but must write your own answers. :contentReference[oaicite:20]{index=20}

---

# Class policies

## Academic honesty
Any instance of apparent academic dishonesty will be reported to Student Judicial Affairs. Students are expected to adhere to the UC Davis Code of Academic Conduct (https://ossja.ucdavis.edu/code-academic-conduct). If found to have cheated on an exam, you will receive an "F" in the course. If found to have cheated on an assignment worth more than 2% of the course grade, you will receive one whole letter grade less than your final course score would have indicated. :contentReference[oaicite:21]{index=21}

## ChatGPT (and variants)
ChatGPT and other LLMs may be used as learning tools, with cautions regarding accuracy and preparation for in-class exams. :contentReference[oaicite:22]{index=22}

## Sharing course content
Course materials are protected by copyright and university policy; students may not reproduce/distribute/display (post/upload) materials without express prior written consent. :contentReference[oaicite:23]{index=23}

## Devices
Cell phones must be put away during lecture except for polling; tablets laid flat are OK; laptops are OK if used only for course-relevant work. :contentReference[oaicite:24]{index=24}

## Email policy
Please include “100B” in the subject line. I will try to answer within 24 hours. Some emails may be answered by directing you to careful perusal of lecture notes or the syllabus. :contentReference[oaicite:25]{index=25}

---

# Course outline (tentative, subject to revision)

| Week | Main Concepts | Perloff Readings |
|------|---------------|------------------|
| Week 1 | Introduction; Monopoly (profit maximization, market power, welfare effects) | 11.1–11.2 |
| Week 2 | Monopoly (pricing, welfare); Price Discrimination | 11.3–11.5; 12.1–12.3 |
| Week 3 | Monopsony; Static Game Theory (normal-form games, Nash equilibrium) | 11.7; 13.1 |
| Week 4 | Cournot Oligopoly; Product Differentiation | 14.1–14.3; 14.5–14.6 |
| Week 5 | Dynamic Game Theory; Stackelberg Competition | 13.3; 14.4 |
| Week 6 | Midterm Exam; Externalities (Pigouvian taxation, Coase theorem) | 17.1–17.2 |
| Week 7 | Externalities (policy design); Public Goods | 17.3–17.4; 17.6 |
| Week 8 | Choice Under Uncertainty (expected utility, risk aversion, insurance) | 16.1–16.5 |
| Week 9 | Asymmetric Information (Adverse Selection and Moral Hazard) | 18.1–18.5; 19.1–19.6 |
| Week 10 | Wrap-up and Final Exam Preparation | — |
